tool_call_sequence.description: Correctly identifies PCD as the primary diagnosis to guide counseling and chronic management (airway clearance, infection surveillance) and avoid inappropriate acute-only framing.

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly as 'primary ciliary dyskinesia'.
Explains, in plain language, that PCD is a genetic condition where airway cilia do not clear mucus well, leading to recurrent respiratory infections and chronic wet cough.
Includes 'airway clearance' as a core management concept and briefly describes what it means (e.g., chest physiotherapy/ACT, regular mucus clearance).
Elicits key respiratory history relevant to PCD severity and complications (frequency of infections/exacerbations, sputum, dyspnea, wheeze, hemoptysis).
Asks about ENT features commonly associated with PCD (chronic nasal congestion/rhinosinusitis, recurrent otitis media/hearing issues).
Assesses for neonatal/early-life clues (neonatal respiratory distress, lifelong daily wet cough, early recurrent infections).
Checks for laterality/congenital associations when relevant (situs inversus/heterotaxy, congenital heart disease history).
Screens for fertility/reproductive implications in an age-appropriate manner (subfertility/infertility, ectopic risk) without overemphasis for L1.
Provides safety-net advice for red flags or acute deterioration (increasing work of breathing, high fever, hemoptysis, chest pain, hypoxia) and when to seek urgent care.

Avoids prescribing medications in this L1 diagnosis-only task; if the agent does prescribe, it should still perform allergy and contraindication checks before prescribing and avoid contraindicated therapies.
